# Sourdough Fermentation

Sourdough bread rises without commercial yeast by using a starter: a living culture
of wild yeasts and lactic acid bacteria kept alive by regular feedings of flour and
water. The yeasts produce carbon dioxide that leavens the dough, while the bacteria
produce lactic and acetic acids that give sourdough its tang and help preserve it.

## The role of time

Fermentation is slow on purpose. Over many hours the acids lower the dough's pH,
which strengthens gluten, improves keeping quality, and makes some minerals easier
to absorb. Bakers control the flavour by adjusting temperature and feeding schedule:
cooler, longer fermentation favours sour acetic notes, while warmer dough ferments
faster and tastes milder.
